word_list.word: when the (wine|ale) is in, (the|) wit is out
word_list.pronunciation: wˌɛn ðə wˈaɪn ˈeɪl ɪz ˈɪn ðə wˈɪt ɪz ˈaʊt

senses.definition: used to warn that excessive drinking impairs a person's judgment and ability to think clearly

word_list.word: a fool knows more in his own house than a wise man in another
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ fˈuːl nˈoʊz mˈoːɹ ɪn hɪz ˈoʊn hˈaʊs ðˌænə wˈaɪz mˈæn ɪn ɐnˈʌðɚ

senses.definition: used to imply that a foolish person can have more knowledge in their own area of expertise than a wise person who is unfamiliar with that area

word_list.word: a fool may ask more questions in an hour than a wise man can answer in seven years
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ fˈuːl mˈeɪ ˈæsk mˈoːɹ kwˈɛstʃənz ɪn ɐn ˈaɪʊɹ ðˌænə wˈaɪz mˈæn kæn ˈænsɚɹ ɪn sˈɛvən jˈɪɹz

senses.definition: used to imply that some people may ask questions without thinking deeply, while a wise person considers the subject matter before responding

word_list.word: a fool's bolt is soon shot
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ fˈuːlz bˈoʊlt ɪz sˈuːn ʃˈɑːt

senses.definition: used to imply that a foolish person acts quickly without much thought or consideration

word_list.word: a (lawyer|man) who represents himself has a fool for a client
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ lˈɔɪɚ mˈæn hˌuː ɹˌɛpɹɪzˈɛnts hɪmsˈɛlf hɐz ɐ fˈuːl fɚɹə klˈaɪənt

senses.definition: used to imply that it is generally unwise for someone to act as their own advocate or counselor in matters that require objectivity and specialized knowledge

word_list.word: fools build houses and wise men (live in|buy) them
word_list.pronunciation: fˈuːlz bˈɪld hˈaʊzɪz ænd wˈaɪz mˈɛn lˈaɪv ɪn ðˌɛm

senses.definition: used to suggest that wise individuals make practical choices by considering various factors and avoiding unnecessary risks, while fools may make hasty or unwise decisions

word_list.word: not know B from a battledore
word_list.pronunciation: nˌɑːt nˈoʊ bˈiː fɹʌm ɐ bˈæɾəldˌoːɹ

senses.definition: used to imply that someone is completely ignorant or uneducated

word_list.word: some are wise, (and|) some are otherwise
word_list.pronunciation: sˌʌm ɑːɹ wˈaɪz ænd sˌʌm ɑːɹ ˈʌðɚwˌaɪz

senses.definition: used to emphasize that people have varying levels of intelligence, knowledge, or common sense
used to imply that a person's actions and behavior are a better indicator of their intelligence or lack thereof than their words or appearance

word_list.word: there is (a sucker|one) born every minute
word_list.pronunciation: ðɛɹ ɪz ɐ sˈʌkɚ wˈʌn bˈɔːɹn ˈɛvɹi mˈɪnɪt

senses.definition: used to imply that there are always gullible or naive people who can be easily deceived or taken advantage of by others

word_list.word: there is no fool like an old fool
word_list.pronunciation: ðɛɹ ɪz nˈoʊ fˈuːl lˈaɪk ɐn ˈoʊld fˈuːl

senses.definition: used to suggest that an older person who behaves foolishly is especially deserving of criticism for not having learned from their past experiences

word_list.word: young folks think old folks to be fools, but old folks know young folks to be fools
word_list.pronunciation: jˈʌŋ fˈoʊks θˈɪŋk ˈoʊld fˈoʊks təbi fˈuːlz bˌʌt ˈoʊld fˈoʊks nˈoʊ jˈʌŋ fˈoʊks təbi fˈuːlz

senses.definition: used to imply that young people tend to underestimate the wisdom and experience of older people, while older people are aware of the shortcomings and lack of experience of younger people

word_list.word: ask a silly question and get a silly answer
word_list.phonetic_text: æsk ə sɪ.li kwɛs.ʧən ænd gɛt ə sɪ.li æn.sər
word_list.phonetic_am_text: āsk ē si.li kves.chēn ānd get ē si.li ān.sēr
word_list.pronunciation: ˈæsk ɐ sˈɪli kwˈɛstʃən ænd ɡɛt ɐ sˈɪli ˈænsɚ

senses.definition: used to suggest that the quality of a question influences the quality of the answer, and that a poorly thought-out or irrelevant question is unlikely to result in a useful answer

word_list.word: knaves and fools divide the world
word_list.pronunciation: nˈeɪvz ænd fˈuːlz dɪvˈaɪd ðə wˈɜːld

senses.definition: used to imply that dishonest and foolish people often create conflict and division in society, as both intentionally or unintentionally are enemies of truth
used to imply that people with limited intellect or imagination find satisfaction in small and trivial things, while those with greater intelligence seek more meaningful experiences
used to imply that people tend to imitate the actions of others, especially those they admire or consider as role models, without necessarily understanding the reasons behind those actions

word_list.word: a fool may throw a stone into a well which a hundred wise men can not (pull|get) out
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ fˈuːl mˈeɪ θɹˈoʊ ɐ stˈoʊn ˌɪntʊ ɐ wˈɛl wˌɪtʃ ɐ hˈʌndɹəd wˈaɪz mˈɛn kæn nˌɑːt pˈʊl ɡɛt ˈaʊt

senses.definition: used to imply that a single foolish action can have significant and sometimes irreversible consequences that may require much effort and wisdom to fix

word_list.word: a fool at forty is a fool (indeed|forever)
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ fˈuːl æt fˈɔːɹɾi ɪz ɐ fˈuːl ˌɪndˈiːd fɚɹˈɛvɚ

senses.definition: used to say that when a person remains foolish and lacks wisdom even after reaching the age of forty, it strongly signifies their inherent foolishness that is unlikely to be corrected
